concepts.title: Multiplication operation on a matrix
concepts.conceptBody:
Matrix Multiplication

If $\text{A }={{\left[ {{\text{a}}_{\text{ij}}} \right]}_{\text{mp}}}\quad $

$\text{B }={{\left[ {{\text{b}}_{\text{ij}}} \right]}_{\text{p n }}}\text{ and then C}=\text{AB}={{\left[ {{\text{C}}_{\text{ik}}} \right]}_{\text{mn}}}$

where ${{\mathbf{C}}_{\text{ik}}}=\sum\limits_{j=1}^{\text{p}}{{{\text{a}}_{\text{ij}}}}{{\text{b}}_{\text{jk}}}$

i.e. ${{\text{C}}_{\text{ik}}}={{\text{a}}_{\text{ij}}}{{\text{b}}_{\text{ik}}}+{{\text{a}}_{12}}~{{\text{b}}_{2\text{k}}}+\ldots {{\text{a}}_{\text{ip}}}{{\text{b}}_{\text{pk}}}$

In other words ${{\text{C}}_{\text{ik}}}=$ Sum of the products of nth row of A (having $\text{p}$ elements) with kth column of $\text{B}$ (having $\text{p}$ elements). This is known as row by column multiplication of matrices.

It may be noted that in determinants we have row by row or column by column multiplication.

Illustration 8

If $A={{\left[ \begin{matrix} 1 & -2 & 3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \\ \end{matrix} \right]}_{2\times 3}}$ and $B={{\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 \\ 2 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]}_{3\times 2}}$ compute AB and show that $AB\neq BA$.

Solution: 

A is $2\quad 3$ type and B is $3\quad 2$ type and hence both A Band BA are defined because the number of columns in pre-factor is equal to the number of rows in post-factor.

$\text{AB}=\left[ \begin{matrix} 1.2-2.4+3.2 & 1.3-2.5+3.1 \\ -4.2+2.4+5.2 & -4.3+2.5+5.1 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

$={{\left[ \begin{matrix} 0 & -4 \\ 10 & 3 \\ \end{matrix} \right]}_{2\times 2}}$

$\text{BA}={{\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 \\ 2 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]}_{3\times 2}}{{\left[ \begin{matrix} 1 & -2 & 3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \\ \end{matrix} \right]}_{2\times 3}}$

$={{\left[ \begin{matrix} -10 & 2 & 21 \\ -16 & 2 & 37 \\ -2 & -2 & 11 \\ \end{matrix} \right]}_{3\times 3}}$

Hence AB $\ne \text{BA}$

If $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ be two matrices then their product is defined or in other words $\text{A}$ is conformable to $\text{B}$ for multiplication if the number of columns of $\mathbf{A}$ is the same as the number of rows in B. i.e., If be $\mathbf{m}\quad \mathbf{p}$ and $\mathbf{p}\quad \mathbf{n}$, the matrix A B will be of the type $\mathbf{mn}$. 

Properties of matrix multiplication

(a) Multiplication of matrices is distributive with respect to addition of matrices.

i.e. $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{B}+\mathbf{C})=\mathbf{AB}+\mathbf{AC}$.

(b) Matrix multiplication is associative if conformability is assured.

i.e. $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{BC})=(\mathbf{AB})\mathbf{C}$.

(c) The multiplication of matrices is not always commutative i.e. AB is not always equal to BA.

(d) Multiplication of a matrix A by a null matrix conformable with A for multiplication is a null matrix i.e. AO = O.

In particular if $A$ be a square matrix and $O$ be square null matrix of the same order, then $\text{AO}=\text{OA}=\text{O}$.

(e) If $\text{AB}=\text{O}$ then it does not necessarily mean that $\text{A}=\text{O}$ or $\text{B}=\text{O}$ or both are $\text{O}$ as shown below.

$\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \\ \end{array} \right]\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ \end{array} \right]$

None of the matrices on the left is a null matrix whereas their product is a null matrix.

(f) Multiplication of matrix A by a unit matrix I: Let $\text{A}$ be a $\text{m}$ n matrix and I be a square unit matrix of order $\text{n}$, so that $A$ and I are conformable for multiplication, then

$\text{A}{{\text{I}}_{\text{n}}}=\text{A}\text{. }$

Similarly for IA to exist $I$ should be square unit matrix of order $m$ and in that case ${{I}_{m}}A=A$
